subject: [WiCS Comp] WiCS LinkedIn Workshop This Friday 🌸
body:
Hi WiCS!

Whether you already have a LinkedIn and are looking to strengthen it or you
are just getting started, this session is designed for you! We will walk
through how to make your profile stand out and actually work for you.

The session will take place this *Friday, April 3rd* from *5 to 6 pm*
in *Harvard
Hall 104.*

We will cover how to craft a strong profile, optimize your experience
section, and use LinkedIn strategically for networking and opportunities.

This event *counts as an educational event for comp*, so it is a great
opportunity to get one in if you are finishing your comp requirements.

Come by, bring your laptop, and leave with a profile you feel confident
about.

WiCS love <3

[wics-chatter] Room/sublet in Porter Square — lease starting June
body:
Hi! I'm looking for a new roommate to start in June (summer sublets also
ok).

It's a very spacious apartment in Porter Square with its own living area,
bedroom, bathroom and non-coin operated washer/dryer set (so essentially
like a studio apt). This would be a great place to live if you're doing
research over the summer or staying in Boston long term.

   - Rent: $1400/mo
   - 6min from the nearest T station, Star Market, restaurants
   (Gustazo/Yume/Sugar and Spice), and ~25min to MIT
   - Other roommate is a current RA at the Broad, she's starting grad
   school at Stanford this Aug :)

 I'm happy to provide more details, show you around, or hop on a call if
you're interested!

Amulya

Sanctuary City is an intimate play by Pulitzer Prize finalist Martyna Majok
about friendship, trust, and the sacrifices we make for the people we love.
In post-9/11 Newark, teenagers B and G hatch a plan to get married so B can
gain citizenship, all while navigating tests, prom, and the universal
stressors and joys of adolescence. But as time moves forward and the stakes
grow higher, their plan threatens to fracture the closest relationship
either of them has ever had. Poignant and deeply human, Sanctuary City
explores how we can create a better world when institutions fail us.

subject: [Bacch-talk] Do you have 5-10 bucks for a great cause?
body:
Hellowell,

I hope all is well. I am so excited to run the 2026 Boston Marathon on
April 20. To do so, I am raising money for the *Brigham and Women's
Stepping Strong Foundation*, a trauma research organization that is close
to my heart. I was at the finish line during the 2013 bombings -- you can
read more about it on my fundraising page
<https://events.brighamandwomens.org/site/TR/Events/BostonMarathonRebrand?pg=personal&px=1218249&fr_id=1500>
(and see me and my family in the first thirty seconds of *American Manhunt* on
Netflix).

I have a big fundraising deadline on Monday! Woohoo! So, if you are
interested in donating or learning more about the cause, please check out
my page or email me with any questions.

Thanks for taking the time to read this message and thanks in advance for
your generosity. Go BIG BLUE!

Lowell Love,

Jack Burton

subject: [Bacch-talk] Free egg tarts! Join Professor Kuriyama for a Discussion on Traditional Medicine
body:
*Why Traditional Medicine Matters, Now, More than Ever*

We're thrilled to invite you to a special event co-hosted by CSA and HEAL
(Health Equity by Asian Literacy). Come join us for *egg tarts(yum!)* and a
conversation with one of Harvard's most distinguished scholars at the
intersection of culture, history, and medicine. RSVP HERE
<https://harvardcsa.us17.list-manage.com/track/click?u=da96a4dbd42fdc0e5ce64f93c&id=3e50a4a6e2&e=d93156692a>
!

*Professor Shigehisa Kuriyama *is the Reischauer Institute Professor of
Cultural History in the Department of East Asian Languages and
Civilizations. His landmark 1999 book, The Expressiveness of the Body and
the Divergence of Greek and Chinese Medicine, won the 2001 William H. Welch
Medal from the American Association for the History of Medicine.

If you are interested in pursuing a career in the health sector or
traditional medicine, this discussion will explore why traditional East
Asian medicine is a vital lens for shaping the future of healthcare.

🗓 March 31st, 4 pm
 Fong Auditorium

HEAL is a new initiative under the Harvard Undergraduate Premedical Society
dedicated to building a community that empowers AAPI pre-medical students
and amplifies Asian and Asian-American voices in healthcare. This event is
open to all who are curious!

-- 
*May Ng*
Harvard College | Class of 2027
A.B. Candidate in Computational Neuroscience

  IFM-Cqp0eAECX1toQzp3

curl -X 'POST' \
  'https://api.k2think.ai/v1/chat/completions' \
  -H 'accept: application/json' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer {your-api-key}' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \

subject: [HRCSA] What Can Traditional Medicine Teach Us Today? A Conversation with Professor Kuriyama
body:
*Why Traditional Medicine Matters, Now, More than Ever*

We're thrilled to invite you to a special event co-hosted by CSA and HEAL
(Health Equity by Asian Literacy). Come join us for *egg tarts(yum!)* and a
conversation with one of Harvard's most distinguished scholars at the
intersection of culture, history, and medicine. RSVP HERE
<https://harvardcsa.us17.list-manage.com/track/click?u=da96a4dbd42fdc0e5ce64f93c&id=3e50a4a6e2&e=d93156692a>
!

*Professor Shigehisa Kuriyama *is the Reischauer Institute Professor of
Cultural History in the Department of East Asian Languages and
Civilizations. His landmark 1999 book, The Expressiveness of the Body and
the Divergence of Greek and Chinese Medicine, won the 2001 William H. Welch
Medal from the American Association for the History of Medicine.

If you are interested in pursuing a career in the health sector or
traditional medicine, this discussion will explore why traditional East
Asian medicine is a vital lens for shaping the future of healthcare.

🗓 March 31st, 4 pm
 Fong Auditorium

HEAL is a new initiative under the Harvard Undergraduate Premedical Society
dedicated to building a community that empowers AAPI pre-medical students
and amplifies Asian and Asian-American voices in healthcare. This event is
open to all who are curious!

Love,
CSA

subject: Stat 111: HW 7 (and chess)
body:
Course: Stat 111
Author: Joe Blitzstein
Link:   https://edstem.org/us/courses/88758/discussion/7861072

Dear Stat 111ers,

HW 7 has been posted. It focuses on hypothesis testing but as usual it also is connected with and reinforces many earlier concepts. 

Q4, about win streaks and cheating allegations in chess, has personal significance to me. I am the faculty advisor to the chess club. Our colleague Mark Glickman is also a chess player and invented a widely-used chess ratings system, Glicko (also used for ratings in various other sports and games, as an alternative to Elo). 

The grandmaster Daniel Naroditsky (an extremely strong player but also a great teacher and popular YouTuber) visited the Harvard chess club two years ago.

Not long after that, he started getting bullied by Kramnik (former world champion) with absurd insinuations of cheating. Last year he reached out to me for a statistician's help on using the data to show that there was no evidence of cheating. Even though I was swamped at the time, I liked him so much and was so outraged about the allegations that I wrote a very positive response, but then I never heard back from him again. I don't think it's known if it was suicide or an accidental overdose, but he was clearly spiraling from having his childhood hero attack him like that and he died 5 months ago, age 29:

https://www.npr.org/2025/10/23/nx-s1-5584161/chess-fide-investigation-naroditsky-kramnik

Best,

Joe

subject: [PBHA Open] Calling all CAs and Labor Allies: Vote in HGSU's Strike Authorization Vote!!
body:
Hello, FUP and PBHA, and happy Housing Day!

I am passing a message along on behalf of the Student Labor Action Movement
(SLAM) and the Harvard Graduate Student Union (HGSU). This is a pressing
matter that affects many academic workers on our campus and every single
student, so we hope you'll give it your full attention:

Dear Harvard College student,

You may have heard that we, the Harvard Graduate Student Union, are voting
to authorize a strike. This means that we’re asking our members whether
they would permit us to strike at some point later this term. We opened the
vote because, after a year of negotiations, Harvard has hardly moved
on the four
key demands <https://harvardgradunion.org/bargaining-2/> we have for our
third contract: stronger protections for non-citizen student workers,
stronger protections against harassment and discrimination, greater union
security, and better pay that’s comparable to the pay earned at rival
institutions like Northwestern and Stanford.

We’re fighting for your fellow undergrad peers, who serve as course
assistants, and for your teaching fellows. These workers deserve stable,
sustainable working and living conditions. They’ll be better equipped to
support your learning when we win a fair third contract.

You can help. Urge Course Assistants to sign a membership card
<https://harvardgradunion.org/join/> to join our union’s movement. Within
48 hours of joining, they’ll be sent a ballot to participate in the strike
authorization vote, whic

subject: [HYP] HYP 391 Greater Exec Elections
body:
Hi all,

As we wrap up the yearbook for 390, it is time to start the process of
*electing
the 391 Greater Exec members*. I encourage anyone who is returning to HYP
next year to consider applying for Greater Exec - serving on Greater Exec
has offered me the opportunity to learn more about the other boards and
meet more people in the yearbook beyond my own board. It was one of the
most rewarding experiences that I've ever had in my college experience, and
we're always on the lookout for more people to be engaged as a part of our
Greater Exec board!

I've attached a document detailing each of the positions and their job
descriptions below separated out by board. Please feel free to reach out to
any of the people listed at the top of the application if you would like to
learn more about what they do!

Application Link:
https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSevGeB9NYZfRHA7YxsUToP9eaCp0KfsOdjeu0JS2YQmqRxgSQ/viewform?usp=dialog

The timeline for the Greater Exec applications are as follows:

1.* Wednesday, April 1, 2026 11:59PM: *Deadline for greater exec
applications to be submitted.
2. *Saturday, April 4, 2026:* In-Person Interviews - all day in office (15
minute interview). Time slots will be emailed and GCal'd to everyone on
Thursday, April 2 based on the availability you provide in the application.

Please let me or any of the other Greater Exec members know if you have any
questions or would like to request more information.

Best,
Nicholas

subject: Opportunity with HUFAC (Flying and Aerospace Club)
body:
Hey everybody,

Forwarding a message from the Flying and Aerospace club (they fly planes!)

--

Hi everyone,

We’re excited to invite you to the first official meeting of the Harvard
Undergraduate Flying & Aerospace Club!

At this meeting, we’ll outline our vision and goals for the club, including
upcoming opportunities to *get students flying*, as well as *factory visits
and industry exposure* in aerospace. Whether you’re already a pilot or just
curious about aviation and space, we’d love to have you involved.

We’re also honored to welcome *Daniel E. Hastings* as our first guest
speaker, perhaps the most accomplished aerospace engineer of our time.
Professor Hastings *leads the AeroAstro department at MIT and is the former
Chief Scientist of the U.S. Air Force*. He’ll be sharing insights from his
work across aerospace engineering, policy, and research, followed by an
open Q&A.

*Details:*

   - 📍 Location: Harvard Hall 202
   - 🕓 Time: 4:30 PM – 5:30 PM
   - 📅 Date: Tuesday, March 31

This is a great opportunity to learn more about the club, connect with
others interested in aviation and aerospace, and hear from an incredible
speaker.

Hope to see you there!

All the best,
Finn and Michael - Co-presidents
Harvard Undergraduate Flying & Aerospace Club

--

Happy flying!
-- 
Chloe Bednar
Chief Engineer, UAV Team
Harvard Undergraduate Aerospace Collective
[image: image.png]

--
